A research program is built on three assumptions at once.

First, it treats idea generation and criticism as processes that can happen mostly in language. But in this world, it is also true that at least some major discoveries do not fit that mold: Einstein’s route from a qualitative insight to field equations required a decade of mathematical work, and that path may not be approximable by verbal search. So the program’s search space excludes or badly compresses an important class of discovery. It can talk around such discoveries, but there is no guarantee it can traverse the actual path that makes them real.

Second, it evaluates explanations through layered filters of quality. But some important ideas, when first proposed, looked confused, ugly, or defective by the standards then in force. Therefore the filters do not merely remove noise; they also risk removing the rare signals that initially resemble noise. The cleaner and earlier the filtering, the more likely the system is to prefer ideas that fit current standards over ideas that would later revise those standards.

Third, it invokes “hard to vary” as a criterion. But in this same world, there is no resolved way to score that property without importing human background knowledge. So the scoring function cannot be neutral. Either it leaves “hard to vary” vague and unusable, or it encodes hidden assumptions supplied by people. In either case, the criterion does not independently ground judgment; it inherits the prejudices and tacit ontology of its designers.

Put together, the program tends to search in a linguistic space, judge by present-looking coherence, and score by concealed human priors. That means it is structurally conservative in two ways: it favors what can already be said, and among what can be said, it favors what already looks respectable.

Now add the remaining facts. Afterwards, trim control over the inexplicable smuggled goods returned. If there was an attempt to regulate what crossed the boundary—what counted as admissible, explainable, or properly scored—that control is reduced only after the goods are already back inside. So the system’s boundary management fails asymmetrically: things that should have been excluded can re-enter under cover of inexplicability, and oversight weakens after the fact rather than before it.

The tape made my ancestor insist that the preacher honor the mortgage. A recording functions as external evidence that compels enforcement of an obligation across authority and lineage. In this thought experiment, that means hidden commitments can be exposed by traces that are not the official scoring criteria themselves. When the overt framework cannot justify its own judgments without smuggling in background assumptions, some “tape” is needed to show what was actually underwriting the decision. Then an inherited authority—the ancestor insisting to the preacher—demands payment on debts the system preferred not to name.

So the world implied by the premises is one where the discovery engine presents itself as principled and explainable, yet depends on unformalized inheritance, misses some mathematically essential routes, suppresses initially ugly truths, and is periodically forced by external records to acknowledge obligations it had concealed.
